coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of poly (aspartic) acid functionalized graphene quantum dots based FRET sensor for selective and sensitive detection of MAGE-A11 antigen
coredata.dc:creator: Shankar Tade, Rahul
Onkar Patil, Pravin
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, we investigated a label-free fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based poly (aspartic) acid (PAsP) functionalized graphene quantum dot (PAsP-GQDs) immunosensor for the selective and sensitive sensing of MAGE-A11 antigen. Synthesis of GQDs was accomplished with hydrothermal carbonization of Onion hull (ONI-GQDs). Synthesized PAsP-GQDs were characterized for fluorescence performance, functional compositions and morphological analysis. To form a typical FRET system, functionalized GQDs conjugated with anti-MAGE-A11 (antibody) using EDC/NHS chemistry served as an energy donor, while graphene nanosheets served as energy acceptors (quenchers). The fabricated PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab immunosensor demonstrated high selectivity to MAGE-A11 with a broader detection range about of 0.05 ng mL−1 – 5 μg mL−1 (R2 = 0.9906) and a detection limit of about of 5.6 pg mL−1, with a rapid response time of 12 min. The performance of the developed immunosensor was established using real sample analysis, which showed an average recovery of 96.8 % with % RSD 0.71 indicating the high precision and reproducibility of the method. Furthermore, to implicate the post-functionalization modifications, cellular bioimaging potential and cytotoxicity studies were conducted as a comparative assessment. The present immunosensing strategies can be utilized as an analytical tool for detecting MAGE-A11 in various cancers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Poly (aspartic) acid functionalization
MAGE-A11
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Turn “on–off-on” sensing
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of ONI-GQDs and PAsP-GQDs synthesis
Optical characterization of ONI-GQDs and PAsP-GQDs
Establishment MAGE-11 immunosensor and measurement of surface interactions
Selectivity and reproducibility of MAGE-11 immunosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the SERB-DST, Government of India (ECR/2017/000905) for research funding and HRPIPER, Shirpur, for providing the required facilities. We are thankful to IIT Madras for the fluorescence decay study. We are grateful to Asbury carbons for providing a gift sample of graphene nanoplates. We are also thankful to IIT Roorkee for providing XPS analysis and STIC Cochin for other characterization facilities. We are also thankful to Averin Biotech Pvt. Ltd. for providing cytotoxicity and cellular bioimaging facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pictures of PAsP-GQDs under the natural light and UV light sources (a) and UV–vis absorbance spectra of ONI-GQDs (b) and PAsP-GQDs (c).

Excitation/emission spectra of PAsP-GQDs (a), wavelength-dependent excitation/ emission spectra of PAsP-GQDs (b-c), fluorescence life-time analysis of ONI-GQDs and PAsP-GQDs, the effect of solvent on the fluorescent properties of PAsP-GQDs, Zeta potential analysis of different immunosensor components (anti-MAGE-A11 (g), PAsP-GQDS@EDC/NHS (h), PAsP-GQDs@Ab (i). UV–vis study of PAsP-GQDs after conjugation with antibody (Blue line) (j), Activated PAsP-GQDs with EDC/NHS (Red line), PAsP-GQDs@Ab after addition of GrN (Black line) and changes in fluorescence intensity at different interfaces of immunosensors components (k).

HR-TEM analysis of PAsP-GQDs (a-b), SAED pattern (c), FESEM-EDX analysis (d), FT-IR analysis of PAsP-GQDs (black line), PAsP-GQDs after addition of EDC/NHS solution (Green line) and PAsP-GQDs after conjugation with anti-MAGE-A11. FT-IR analysis of ONI-GQDs (a), XPS analysis of ONI-GQDs (a) and PAsP-GQDs.
A schematic depicting the synthesis of PAsP-GQDs synthesis and working of FRET-based sensing system for MAGE-A11 antigen.

Fluorescence quenching spectra of PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab with increasing concentrations GrN (a), fluorescence quenching efficiency of GrN with varying concentration (b), fluorescence recovery spectra of PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab/GrN complex with increasing concentration of MAGE-A11 and Real sample analysis of PAsP-GQDs@MAGE-A11 immunosensor (e-f).

(a) Cellular uptake/bioimaging study of ONI-GQDs and PAsP-GQDs using confocal microscopy with comparative differences in SW-1271 cell lines. DIC images (a, f), ONI-GQDs (b-d), PAsP-GQDs (g-i) and merged images (e, j). (b) MTT assay of ONI-GQDs and PAsP-GQDs.

row.entry: 5 fg mL−1 – 50 ng mL−1 for both analytes
electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV)

head.ce:title: Fabrication of poly (aspartic) acid functionalized graphene quantum dots based FRET sensor for selective and sensitive detection of MAGE-A11 antigen

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur - 425405, (MS) India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur - 425405, (MS) India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, we investigated a label-free fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based poly (aspartic) acid (PAsP) functionalized graphene quantum dot (PAsP-GQDs) immunosensor for the selective and sensitive sensing of MAGE-A11 antigen.

Synthesis of GQDs was accomplished with hydrothermal carbonization of Onion hull (ONI-GQDs). Synthesized PAsP-GQDs were characterized for fluorescence performance, functional compositions and morphological analysis. To form a typical FRET system, functionalized GQDs conjugated with anti-MAGE-A11 (antibody) using EDC/NHS chemistry served as an energy donor, while graphene nanosheets served as energy acceptors (quenchers). The fabricated PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab immunosensor demonstrated high selectivity to MAGE-A11 with a broader detection range about of 0.05 ng mL−1 – 5 μg mL−1 (R2 = 0.9906) and a detection limit of about of 5.6 pg mL−1, with a rapid response time of 12 min. The performance of the developed immunosensor was established using real sample analysis, which showed an average recovery of 96.8 % with % RSD 0.71 indicating the high precision and reproducibility of the method. Furthermore, to implicate the post-functionalization modifications, cellular bioimaging potential and cytotoxicity studies were conducted as a comparative assessment. The present immunosensing strategies can be utilized as an analytical tool for detecting MAGE-A11 in various cancers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The global cancer risk is growing steadily, with a high fatality rate per year. The most recent epidemiological survey statistics raised concerns about the possibility of imposing cancer cases [1]. Among the various types of cancer, lung cancer (LC) is the most frequently diagnosed cancer worldwide next to breast cancer [2]. With precise early detection testing, the stages of LC can be identified. In that, identification of different tumor markers could be the key finding to arrest at the proper stage with desirable treatment. Different types of tumor marker (protein) were identified as an indication of the LC such as carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), cancer antigen-125, pro-gastrin-releasing peptide (proGRP), cytokeratin fragment (CYFRA 21–1), Melanoma-associated antigen family proteins (MAGE’s) and neuron-specific enolase (NSE) etc. [3,4]. Amongst these, MAGEs are often observed in the fetal keratinocytes, placenta, and male germ cells as well as different human malignancies. Melanoma-associated antigen-A11 (MAGE-A11) is an X-linked gene that is expressed at a lower rate specifically in the placenta, testis, endometrium and ovary of humans [5]. The MAGEs family protein plays a vital role in physiology as well as pathology of germ cell development embryogenesis, neurogenetic, cell cycle progression, apoptosis, etc. MAGE-A11 antigen expression is also found at differential levels in lung cancer, breast cancer and prostate cancer [6]. Surprisingly, it was found at higher levels in adenocarcinoma than in squamous cell carcinoma of lungs. Hence, MAGE family antigens are considered ideal target markers for immunotherapy as well as early diagnosis and management [7,8].

Accordingly, the use of different methods such as Southern blotting with reproductive tract fluid (RTF), polymerase chain reaction-based (PCR) based methods, high-resolution telomere length analysis (STELA), DNA-Microarrays, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), etc. being investigated as supportive methods [9]. Though the systematic estimation methodology of MAGEs is limited, there is growing interest in researchers for the assessment of their interactions with different proteins owing to quantifying their concentration in various body fluids [9,10]. A careful and systematic amalgamation of various types of nanomaterials i. e. metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [11], inorganic metal complexes (IMC) [12], carbon-based nanomaterials [13], etc. gained enormous attention in the allied biosensing field. Besides, the application of novel carbon-based nanomaterials with this traditional method is trending amongst researchers [14,15]. Amongst these materials graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as well as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) attained substantial consideration due to their unique photophysical properties [16–18].

In this track, fluorescence or förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), is a fundamental energy transfer process involving fluorescent molecules that has been employed by numerous researchers for the detection of a variety of biomolecules [19–21]. The direct interaction involving a few nanometer ranges induces a transfer of energy at the molecular level, which aids in the fabrication of the sensing probe [22]. The detection of various cancer biomarkers has been accounted for using FRET, such as Human epididymis protein 4 (HE4) [21], Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) [23], cancer antigen 125 and other biomolecules [24]. Recently, the ideation involving the use of CDs and GQDs in sensing has been expanded with a curious use of available surface functional groups using different polymeric materials [25], dopants [26,27] and poly-amino compounds [28,29]. Surface modification of GQDs or CDs provides the links for additional attachments or can improve the sensitivity or selectivity towards a specific compound of interest [30].

In the present work, we have explored the one-step hydrothermal synthesis of poly (aspartic) acid (PAsP) functionalized graphene quantum dots (PAsP-GQDs) and ONI GQDs. Utilizing the controlled heating, both the GQDs have been accomplished using onion hull waste. To the best of our information, this is the first report on the use of PAsP-GQDs for the FRET-based detection of MAGE-A11 with high sensitivity and selectivity. The establishment of FRET-based systems was done using graphene nanosheets (GrN) as quencher (energy acceptor) and PAsP-GQDs conjugated antibody as a donor, while MAGE-A11 was used for recovery of fluorescence. PAsP functionalization offered a higher cellular uptake in the SW-1271 cell lines confirmed by bioimaging experiments.

All the Reagents, characterization instruments and analytical equipment used for the physicochemical characterization of GQDs are specified in the Supplementary Material
(S-1 and S-2).

For the preparation of ONI-GQDs and PAsP-GQDs hydrothermal method was adopted. In this, ONI-GQDs prepared with high-temperature hydrothermal carbonization and PAsP-GQDs were accomplished with the low-temperature hydrothermal treatment of ONI-GQDs with PAsP. In brief, weathered Onions hulls (Allium cepa) were acquired from a local market (Shirpur), washed thoroughly with DI water, and then dried for 24 h at 60 °C in a hot air oven. The dried hull was then grounded enough to a fine powder and sieved through a 40# mesh size [31]. Then, 1 g of powder was dispersed in 50 mL of a beaker containing DI water with 10 % ethanol. Next, the beaker was wrapped with paper the mixture was kept for ultrasonication for 20 min.

The beaker was further kept for vigorous stirring on a heating plate at 50–60 °C for 30 min to evaporate ethanol. The resultant mixture was transferred in a Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave and allowed to complete the reaction at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling the instrument at room temperature, the obtained dispersion was then transferred into a beaker and further ultrasonicated for 20 min. To remove the remaining carbonaceous material, the obtained blackish-brown dispersion was strained through a 0.22 mm microporous membrane. Further, the obtained liquid was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane for 24 h against DI water. Next, a suitable quantity of the dialyzed solutions was dried on a heating plate at about 60 °C to get dry product and the remainder was kept at 4 °C for further characterization. To synthesize PAsP-GQDs, 20 mg of ONI-GQDs powder was added to a beaker containing 20 mL DI water with pH 8 (adjusted by 1 M NaOH). The dispersion was transferred in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 8 h. Next, the obtained dispersion was rinsed through a dialysis membrane of (MWCO, 12,000 kD) for 12 h. Similarly, a suitable quantity of the dialyzed solutions was dried in an oven at about 60 °C to get dry product and the remainder was kept at 4 °C for further characterization.
The fluorescence studies were carried out using a 0.1 mg/mL solution of ONI-GQDs and PAsP-GQDs throughout the experiments.

In a typical method, 1 gm graphite nanoplatelets powder was taken in a 50 mL capacity beaker containing H2SO4/H3PO4 (4:1) with 20 mL water and the mixture was kept for ultrasonication for 1 h. Further, obtained dispersion was poured into a Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave and heated for 12 h at 180 °C. The obtained dispersion was then neutralized with 1 M NaOH solution. Next, the resultant dispersion was further ultrasonicated for 1 h and dialyzed via dialysis membrane (MWCO 12,000) to obtain a uniformly dispersed GrN solution.
The human lung carcinoma cell lines (SW-1271) were acquired from National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS, Pune). The detailed methodology has been presented in S-3.

In an Eppendorf tube (1.5 mL capacity) containing 0.5 μL (0.5 mg mL−1) of anti-MAGE-11 a 50 μL of NHS (10 mM) was supplemented and vigorously stirred for 2 min. Subsequently, 50 μL of EDC (30 mM) was added and further stirred for 5 min. As mentioned above, the activated anti-MAGE-11 solution was mixed with a solution of 500 μL PAsP-GQDs solution (pre-activated by EDC 10 mM and 5 mM HNS) and incubated in a humid environment for 20 min at 37 °C to allow conjugate formation, named as PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab. Afterward, the anti-MAGE-11 conjugated PAsP-GQDs solution (PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab) was subjected to ultra-centrifuge for washing and removing unbound antibodies. The pre-activated PAsP-GQDs, anti-MAGE-11 and PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab were assessed using zeta potential study, FT-IR and UV–vis analysis[32].

The target antigen was detected using changes in PL intensity after the addition of a specific concentration of quencher (graphene nanosheets) and re-addition of antigen ensuing the regain of fluorescence. Herein, 400 μL of antibody functionalized GQDs solution (PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab) mixed with GrN dispersion (0.1 mg mL−1). Following this, different concentrations of MAGE-A11 (0.0 ng to 100 ng mL−1 and 0.5 μg to 5 μg mL−1) were added to the PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab/GrN solution and allowed to incubate few minutes and the time expended to complete the reaction was recorded [32].

Consequently, the alterations in the PL intensity were inspected at various time intervals. The relationship of PL with the log concentration of MAGE-11 was measured using equation (1).
(1)
Y = M ×
Log
1 0
+ C

The performance of the prepared immunosensor was assessed in human (healthy) serum samples. A similar procedure was used to obtain serum samples, as mentioned in our previous work [32]. Different concentration of MAGE-A11 (0.03 ng mL−1 to 6 ng mL−1) was used for analysis.

The selectivity of the fabricated immunosensor has been performed to assess the selective affinity toward the MAGE-A11 (6 ng mL−1). In this, different proteins such as CEA, BSA, hTERT and egg albumin were selected. When tested in a simulated physiological environment, an ideal immunosensor should be stable. Thus, the prepared biosensor was subjected to different ionic solutions (Fe+, Ca+, Na+, K+) and other compounds with 0.1 M solutions (glucose, folic acid, lactose, urea, and ascorbic acid).

For reproducibility study, four different immunosensor namely MA-11Ab@GQDs-1, MA-11Ab@GQDs-2, MA-11Ab@GQDs-3, and MA-11Ab@GQDs-4 was fabricated same procedure mentioned in section 2.5 and equated for changes in the PL intensity with MAGE-A11 (6 ng mL−1). The relative standard deviation (% RSD) noted was less than 6.4 % indicating the good accuracy and high reproducibility of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of ONI-GQDs and PAsP-GQDs synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Agricultural processing and domestic storage generate a large amount of onion hull waste, which appears to be managed properly. Besides, onion hulls are composed of with high Carbon/Nitrogen ratio with the least amount of Sulphur [33,34]. The use of Onion shell waste was anticipated as a carbonaceous precursor for GQDs synthesis. Before choosing the ideal synthesis parameters, we performed different trials to optimize the different variables such as PAsP concentration, onion shell extract (carbon source) temperature, reaction time, etc. It was observed that the degree of carbonization is directly proportional to the temperature. While high temperature resulted in diminished surface functionalization of GQDs due to degradation of PAsP. Thus following parameters were selected as an optimum synthesis method for PAsP-GQDs.

Hydrothermal decompositions at 180 °C cause structural changes in the content of the onion shells resulting in the rearrangement of carbon core, nitrogen and the very least quantity of sulfur which yields ONI-GQDs doped with nitrogen and sulfur. A plausible mechanism of GQDs formations is illustrated in Fig. 4 a. Similarly, poly (aspartic) acid when undergoes low temperature prolonged hydrothermal carbonization (120 °C) with ONI-GQDs in a slightly basic environment (pH 8), results in the structural rearrangement of functional groups of PAsP [35,36]. The use of PAsP-GQDs signifies the presence of additional carboxylated moieties on the surface offering better surface interactions as well as the stable surface attachment of antigen. The presence of abundant surface carboxyl moiety assists the better photophysical and cellular penetration ability therefore it can be used as cellular bioimaging probes. The changes in the surface morphology, in a comparative sense, have been characterized using different techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of ONI-GQDs and PAsP-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As a comparison, we only discussed the characterization of ONI-GQDs using different characterization techniques where significant structural changes implicated the sensing performances against PAsP-GQDs. The photoluminescent performance of PAsP-GQDs was assessed under different light sources ( Fig. 1
a). The optical properties of synthesized ONI-GQDs and PAsP-GQDs were studied using UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. 1 , b-c). The UV–vis spectra of ONI-GQDs denote a shoulder peak at 219.7 nm and a strong peak at ∼ 273 nm representing the aromatic sp2 (CC) denoting the π-π* transitions, while PAsP-GQDs exhibit typical peaks around 254.4 nm, 277.1 and 326 nm for n-π*, indicating the CO, CN/CN. Besides, a typical CN/CN with a particular n-π* transition denotes the presence of amine functionality on the surface as well as N-doping in the graphitic carbon [37]. The additional peak at about 326 nm signifies the introduction of energy states, which may be due to the presence of S-doping [38].

The PL behaviour of PAsP-GQDs was investigated at various excitation and emission wavelengths, demonstrating excitation/emission independence. The PAsP-GQDs showed maximum excitation at 347 nm while emission was observed at 485 nm ( Fig. 2
-a). Before fabricating the fluorescent immunosensor, the fluorescence lifetime of the PAsP-GQDs is vital to consider for its stability. Therefore, time-resolved PL decay analysis of ONI-GQDs vs PAsP-GQDs was accompanied to assess alterations in fluorescence lifespan following PAsP functionalization. The PL decay of both the GQDs has been stated in Table S1 and calculated using the equation stated in (SI-4). The ONI-GQDs showed a fluorescence lifetime, s = 2.83 ns (transitions 454 nm) and PAsP-GQDs showed a greater lifetime of s = 3.26 (transitions 450) with excitation at 350 nm and emission 480 nm, respectively ( Fig. 2 , d-e). Moreover, the quantum yield (% QY) of prepared ONI-GQDs and PAsP-GQDs was calculated by the formula given in (SI-5). The QY of ONI-GQDs was noted at about ∼ 11.2 % and ∼ 13.6 %, respectively. From the above optical characterizations, it can be deduced that surface functionalization by PAsP significantly enhanced the QY of the GQDs [39,40].

Hydrothermal carbonization of the onion hull structure results in structural changes into intermediate biochar formation which is followed by the carbonized product formation, namely ONI-GQDs. The FT-IR spectra of PAsP-GQDs showed a 3275.1 cm−1 medium stretching representing the –OH, while a peak at 1602 cm−1 signifies the carboxyl group (Fig. 3
e). In ONI-GQDs, the existence of a peak in the range of 3400–3450 cm−1 indicates the –OH stretching. The peaks at 1463 cm−1 and 1381 cm−1 signify the CH bending (alkoxy or epoxy) and sulfur moiety, respectively (Fig. 3 f). Further bands at 2931 signify the CH stretching vibration. The additional band observed at 1621, 1568, and 1418 cm−1 were consigned to carboxylic, in-plane bending vibration NH and COO– of a carboxyl group, respectively [43–45]. The changes in the elemental composition of ONI-GQDs were confirmed by the elemental analysis (SEM-EDX) which showed the existence of carbon = 61.7 %, oxygen = 38.46 % and sulfur at 4.14 % (wt. %) ( Figure S3 ), which significantly reduced after the surface passivation by PAsP with the graphitic structure of PAsP-GQDs to oxygen = 41.7 % and carbon = 55.2 %. The alteration in the atomic percentage is indicative of surface functionalization of PAsP due to additional attachment on the surface [36].

The PAsP-GQDs were studied using XPS analysis to endorse the presence of different elements and post-functionalization changes. The XPS investigation revealed the transformations in the structure of ONI-GQDs concerning PAsP-GQDs after PAsP functionalization ( Fig. 3 -g-h). The survey spectrum of ONI-GQDs shows the existence of a characteristic peak of carbon at 284.1 eV (sp3), oxygen at 532.5 eV and the existence of sulfur at about 199.4 eV instead of carbon and oxygen [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment MAGE-11 immunosensor and measurement of surface interactions
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, it is essential to observe whether there is a FRET mechanism exists or not between the MAGE-A11 antibodies (anti-MAGE-A11) conjugated PAsP-GQDs solution (PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab) after the addition of GrN and its recovery by addition of selective antigen. We studied different literature for the optimum choice of spiked sample. It was anticipated that the selection should have enough concentration that could be detected by the immunosensor ideally [27,32,39,40]. Accordingly, a solution containing activated anti-MAGE-11 (10 µL) was added to the PAsP-GQDs (100 μg mL−1) to form the conjugation of anti-MAGE-11 on the PAsP-GQDs surface and incubated for 20 min. Further, changes in the PL were observed with the addition of (quencher) GrN solution 0.5 µL (0.1 mg mL−1). A marked decline in the intensity was noted due to the establishment of the FRET system, the system required about 10 the quenching of the intensity. The resulting nanocomplex was assessed using UV–vis, FT-IR and ζ-potential study (Fig. 2 j and Fig. 3 e). Successively, 0.2 ng mL−1 of MAGE-A11 was added to the above immunosensor, mixed well and incubated for several minutes at 37 °C to track changes in PL intensity over time ( Figure S6 ). Similarly, about 12 min were required for the retrieval of fluorescence intensity ( Fig. 2 k).

The quenching efficiency of GrN was calculated using equation (2), which was estimated at ∼ 89.64 % at 5 µg mL−1 of GrN concentration. Likewise, the regaining in intensity after the addition of MAGE-11 was calculated at about 86.21 % at 0.5 ng mL−1. The surface reaction of anti-MAGE-11 with PAsP-GQDs resulted in the establishment of a complex that offered the passing of fluorescence signals, while the addition of GrN causes the blockage of signals realized due to energy acceptance. Likewise, UV–vis and FT-IR analysis showed the possible alteration in the functional group due to surface interactions. UV–vis analysis shows the hyperchromic blue shift due to activation of functional groups upon addition of EDC/NHS solution in PAsP-GQDs, while it was further masked after forming conjugation with anti-MAGE-A11. Similarly, the FT-IR analysis endorsed the changes in the position of functional groups after the addition of EDC/NHS solution causes a shift in the PAsP-GQDs from 3275.1 cm−1 to 3423 cm−1 (intense broader hydroxyl group stretching), while the peak shifted from 1602 cm−1 to 1674 cm−1 (carboxyl groups).

Besides, the addition of anti-MAGE-A11 resulted in conjugation with activated functional groups with slight restoration, carboxyl groups were observed around 1629 cm−1 and hydroxyl group was noted at about 3324 cm−1. Additionally, changes in the QY of the PAsP-GQDs were also calculated to assess the surface interactions, bare PAsP-GQDs exhibited a QY of 13.6 % which was after conjugation with anti-MAGE-A11 measured about 12.3 %. On the other hand, after the addition of GrN, the QY reduced to 10.24 % possibly due to a decline in fluorescence intensity and further addition of MAGE-A11 slight increase was noted in QY, which is measured at about 12.86 %.

Literature revealed various mechanism for the detection of different proteins (antigens) using FRET-based mechanism [48,49] π-π stacking interaction [39], fluorescence quenching based sensing [50,51], ratiometric [52] and -photon fluorescence sensing [53], etc. The performance of the proposed PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab sensor can be summarized in the typical three steps; the schematic depiction of the probable mechanism is given in Fig. 4.

Firstly, the fluorescent intensity of donor component PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab is noted at an excitation wavelength of 347 nm which gives a strong cyan-blue fluorescence at 480 nm denoted as the ‘ON” state. Further, the optimized quantity of GrN dispersion (0.1 mg mL−1) was added to the above solution. The electrostatic interaction and π–π stacking interaction (non-covalent interactions) impose surface adsorption resulting in the obstruction in fluorescence signals leading to quenching of fluorescence, designated as the ‘OFF’ state (FRET). Herein, the basic mechanism responsible for FRET is the transfer of its energy which is firstly absorbed by PAsP-GQDs which is relatively high enough for excitation to a nearby acceptor chromophore imparted by PAsP surface groups following the non-radiative transfer fashion over long-range causing dipole–dipole interactions. These interactions ceased when the GrN spiked in the system due to the absorption of energy by PAsP-GQDs causing quenching of fluorescence. Consecutively, the addition of serial concentration of spiked MAGE-11-Ag solution gives rise to the formation of antigen–antibody immunocomplex resulting in the restoration of fluorescence signals (No-FRET) (Fig. 4 b).

The addition of GrN with serial increasing concentration (from 0.01 ng mL−1 to 100 µg mL−1) to the PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab observed the decline in the fluorescence intensity due to the FRET mechanism as mentioned earlier (Fig. 5
, a-b). The quenching proficiency of GrN to PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab was assessed using equation (2);
(2)
Q E =
F 0
- F
F 0
Where; F0 is the intensity of the PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab and F is the intensity of PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab/GrN conjugate.

The overall quenching efficiency of the GrN toward PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab was estimated at 96.7 %. Furthermore, it has been noticed that the PL intensity of MAGE-A11 functionalized PAsP-GQDs was restored with cumulative MAGE-A11 antigen concentration (0.0 ng to 100 ng mL−1 and 0.5 μg to 5 μg mL−1). This restoration in the fluorescence intensity can be linearly correlated to the supplemented concentration and is attributed due to the establishment of the antigen–antibody immunocomplex. Fig. 5 c indicates the fluorescence recovery of the PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab/GrN immunocomplex with serial addition of MAGE-A11 due to the disturbance in the FRET mechanism and disposition of GrN. In this instance, PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab/GrN, and linear correlation are attained within a dynamic range (0.05 ng mL−1 – 5 μg mL−1), ascribed by a regression equation (3).
(3)
I = 0 .
1525 log1
0
MAGE
- A11
ng mL
- 1
+ 1 . 2527
Regression coefficient R2 = 0.9848.

The PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab probe, with a remarkable linear and wide detection range of 0.05 ng mL−1 to 5 µg mL−1 was effectively used for MAGE-A11 antigen sensing with a detection limit of 5.6 pg mL−1 (0.0056 ng mL−1).

As far as the previous reports considered, the present work demonstrated the first report on quantitative estimation of MAGE-A11 antigen. However, a comparative assessment reporting the different MAGE family proteins with our work has been presented in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and reproducibility of MAGE-11 immunosensor

ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the fabricated MAGE-A11 sensing was carried out in PBS containing the human serum samples spiked with five different known concentrations of MAGE-A11 (0.03, 0.7, 1.5, 3 and 6 ng mL−1). The optimum incubation time provided for immunoreaction is 12 min, the same previous sensing step. The results indicate that both the spiked serum sample and standard showed a similar recovery pattern. The PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab fluorescent immunosensor demonstrated an average recovery of 96.8 % with % RSD 0.71 indicating the high precision and reproducibility of the method ( Table S2 ). The plot of fluorescence intensity and the log of the target antigen (MAGE-A11) are given in Fig. 5 , e-f.

The recovery in the spiked serum samples was measured using equation (4) and listed in Table S1 . The fabricated nanoprobe exhibited about 81.05 % average recovery in spiked blood serum samples (R2 = 0.9801).
P e r c e n t a g e
( % )
r e c o v e r y =
Y s
-
Y u
Y s
Where, Ys is the actual MAGE-A11 spiked concentration and Ys, Yu are the acquired concentrations in different spiked and unspiked samples, respectively.

A cellular uptake study in human small cell lung carcinoma cell lines was performed to compare and assess the bioimaging potential of both ONI-GQDs and PASP-GQDs (SW-1271). Fig. 6
a indicates the fluorescence images of ONI-GQDs and PAsP-GQDs recorded with 540 nm (red laser), 488 nm (green laser) and 405 nm (blue laser) of UV light under the confocal laser microscope. Different studies evaluated the surface functionalization and sulfur doping effects on the photophysical properties of GQDs, which are also implicated in cellular uptake [59,60]. Carboxylation and the presence of numerous hydroxyl groups on the surface of PAsP-GQDs may be involved in pH-dependent targeted applications due to their altered solubility (increased lipophilicity), which allows PAsP-GQDs to accumulate in extracellular compartments as well as the nucleus when compared to ONI-GQDs [61,62]. The presence of surface charge and abundant carboxyl groups on PAsP-GQDs surface drives the penetration of cell membrane while the weakly acidic environment of cancer cells offered well accumulation in surrounding organelles such as endoplasmic reticulum, ribosomal region and lysosomes [63–65]. The remarkable fluorescent properties and excellent cellular uptake studies show that PAsP-GQDs have the potential to be used as diagnostic imaging probes in cancer.

Endocytosis (clathrin or caveolae-mediated) and macropinocytosis were discovered in the literature as cellular absorption mechanisms of graphene-based nanostructured materials [66–68]. Besides, the presence of diverse surface functional groups plays an important role in the cellular internalization of such nanomaterials [69,70]. In our findings, ONI-GQDs showed cytotoxic potential with the IC50 concentrations at 99.31 µg mL−1, while PAsP-GQDs exhibited IC50 concentrations at 61.36 µg mL−1 with 24 h of incubation time ( Fig. 6 b). The presence of abundant surface carboxyl functionalization pacifies its strong hydrophobic interaction with SW-121 cells presenting a high internalization capacity, while increased incubation time imposed toxicity [70].

In summary, we developed a one-pot hydrothermal method for the synthesis of PAsP functionalized GQDs from waste onion hull powder. To create a FRET-based system, anti-MAGE-A11 (antibody) was conjugated with PAsP-GQDs to serve as a fluorescence energy donor. Additionally, GrN served as an energy acceptor (quencher) for the fluorescent signals that the MAGE-A11 antigen helped recover. The fabricated PAsP-GQDs@M−A11−Ab immunosensor exhibited high selectivity towards MAGE-A11 offering a broader detection range of 0.05 ng mL−1 – 5 µg mL−1 (R2 = 0.9906) with a detection limit of about of 5.6 pg mL−1. A quick establishment in the energy acceptor and donor system (FRET) contributes to a 12-minute quenching and recovery cycle. Fabricated immunosensor was evaluated for real sample analysis in human serum samples, which showed an average recovery of 96.8 % with % RSD 0.71 demonstrating the great accuracy and reproducibility of the proposed method. The current immunosensing approaches can be used as an analytical tool for detecting MAGE-A11 in various cancers. We believe that further endeavours can be made to integrate this platform for the design of a point-of-care cancer detection system.

Rahul Shankar Tade: Conceptualization, Data curation, Software, Writing - original draft. Pravin Onkar Patil: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the SERB-DST, Government of India (ECR/2017/000905) for research funding and HRPIPER, Shirpur, for providing the required facilities. We are thankful to IIT Madras for the fluorescence decay study. We are grateful to Asbury carbons for providing a gift sample of graphene nanoplates. We are also thankful to IIT Roorkee for providing XPS analysis and STIC Cochin for other characterization facilities. We are also thankful to Averin Biotech Pvt. Ltd. for providing cytotoxicity and cellular bioimaging facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107971.

ce:section.ce:para:
